Essential metals like copper and zinc become toxic above permissible limits while nonessential metals like lead and cadmium exert toxicity even at trace levels.

Cross-kingdom commonalities in PTEs include oxidative stress, enzyme inhibition, genotoxicity, with differences in exposure pathways and trophic transfer, such as mercury biomagnifying up to 10-fold in aquatic food webs.
Mechanistic endpoints are linked to population-level and ecosystem-level impacts.
Phytoremediation can achieve up to 80% removal efficiency for zinc and cadmium.
summary: The paper provides a comprehensive review of potentially toxic elements (PTEs) in plants and animals, integrating their environmental pathways, toxicity mechanisms, and biomonitoring insights across biological kingdoms.

title: Sub-chronic toxicity of an aqueous extract of Epimedium sagittatum (Sieb. Et Zucc.) Maxim. in rats
claims: Sub-chronic toxicity of Epimedium sagittatum aqueous extract in rats leads to organ coefficient changes and injury, with no observed adverse effect level below 7.5 g/kg.
Female rats show liver and pancreas injury along with metabolic dysregulation involving phenylalanine, tyrosine, tryptophan, valine, leucine, and isoleucine metabolism.
Male rats exhibit liver and adrenal gland injury alongside metabolic alterations in valine, leucine, isoleucine biosynthesis and pyruvate metabolism.
summary: The study identifies sub-chronic toxicity effects of Epimedium sagittatum aqueous extract in rats, including organ injury and metabolic dysregulation, highlighting the need for caution in its clinical use.

claims: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) prevents skeletal muscle atrophy induced by cisplatin through redox regulation and modulation of myogenic microRNAs.
